title: Trompt: Towards a Better Deep Neural Network for Tabular Data
authors: Kuan-Yu Chen, Ping-Han Chiang, Hsin-Rung Chou, Ting-Wei Chen, Tien-Hao Chang (National Cheng Kung University, Taiwan; per ICML 2023 author list)
tokenization_method: Per-feature value embedding (same scaffold as FT-Transformer/SAINT) feeding a prompt-learning feature-importance mechanism instead of self-attention. Step 1 (feature embedding, part 2 of the Trompt Cell): each numerical column is embedded via a Dense layer applied to its scalar, each categorical column via an Embedding lookup, then LayerNorm/Concat, producing E_feature in R^{B x C x d} (one d-dim token per column). Step 2 (the 'prompt' tokenizer, the actual contribution): two INPUT-INDEPENDENT trainable matrices are learned -- column embeddings E_column in R^{C x d} (one learned vector per column, sample-invariant 'intrinsic property') and prompt embeddings E_prompt in R^{P x d} (P learned 'soft prompts'). Sample-specific feature importances are derived as M_importance = softmax(hat_SE_prompt (x) SE_column^T) in R^{B x P x C}, a batched matmul (cosine-style similarity) between prompts (fused with the previous cell's output O_prev so input information flows in) and column embeddings, softmaxed over columns. Step 3: E_feature is expanded by a Dense layer to hat_E_feature in R^{B x P x C x d} (so each prompt sees a different feature embedding -- ablation-critical), then the cell output is the column-wise sum of hat_E_feature (.) M_importance in R^{B x P x d}. Trompt stacks L=6 such cells; each cell's output feeds a shared Trompt Downstream (prompt-wise softmax-weighted sum -> 2 Dense layers -> per-cell prediction). Per-cell predictions are summed for the training loss and averaged at inference.
feature_types_handled: numerical and categorical (mixed/heterogeneous). No native missing-value handling (assumes preprocessed input per Grinsztajn45 protocol) and no temporal/time-varying support; rows treated as unordered static feature sets.
token_granularity: per-feature (per-column): one d-dim value-token per column, exactly like FT-Transformer. The genuinely new representational unit is the LEARNED, INPUT-INDEPENDENT column embedding (one per column) and the P prompt embeddings -- these are not tokens of the input but learned codes that the cell uses to assign a per-sample, per-prompt importance weight to each column token.
numerical_strategy: raw-scalar-through-Dense (single linear embedding of the scalar value), explicitly following SAINT/FT-Transformer. NOT piecewise-linear, NOT periodic/Fourier, NOT binned/target-aware. The numerical encoder is deliberately minimal; the contribution is the importance mechanism, not the numerical embedding -- so this is the obvious slot to upgrade (PLR/periodic) when porting.
categorical_strategy: standard learned embedding lookup per categorical column (entity-embedding style), same token dimension d as numerical tokens.
high_cardinality_handling: none specific. Per-column embedding tables grow linearly with cardinality; no hashing, target encoding, or shared/compositional codes. Note Grinsztajn45 deliberately drops/limits high-cardinality categoricals, so the paper neither tests nor addresses this regime.
temporal_handling: none. No patching, recurrence, or static/temporal fusion. Several benchmark datasets (Bike Sharing, nyc-taxi) are temporal in origin but are treated as flat tabular rows.
scales_to_many_features: Weakly addressed and a real concern for the 70+-feature target. The importance step replaces O(C^2) self-attention with a O(P x C) batched matmul (P prompts x C columns), which is cheaper per-feature than full self-attention -- a mild scaling advantage. BUT the architecture is parameter-heavy: P=128 prompts and L=6 cells with d=128, plus the part-3 Dense expanding features to R^{B x P x C x d}, blows up activation memory as B x P x C x d (the P x C x d tensor per sample is materialized). Reported param counts range from ~850K (Grinsztajn datasets, modest C) to 38-60M on SAINT's wider/higher-cardinality datasets -- comparable to or only slightly below SAINT. No explicit complexity analysis or wide-table (hundreds of features) stress test is given; benchmark feature counts are mostly small-to-moderate.
downstream_model: Custom prompt-learning MLP stack (NOT a Transformer): L=6 Trompt Cells, each a 3-part block (importance via prompt-column matmul + feature embedding + Dense expansion), feeding a shared Trompt Downstream (Dense+softmax prompt-weighting -> ReLU MLP -> linear head). Deep-supervision: every cell emits a prediction; losses summed, predictions averaged.
pretraining_objective: none; fully supervised end-to-end. The 'prompt learning' is an architectural analogy (learned soft prompts + fixed-like column embeddings), NOT actual pretraining/transfer -- column and prompt embeddings are trained from scratch per dataset. No self-supervised or target-aware objective.
key_results: Primary benchmark: Grinsztajn45 (45 curated datasets, Grinsztajn et al. 2022), grouped by size (medium/large) and feature type (numerical-only / heterogeneous), metric = accuracy (clf) / r2 (reg), evaluated over a hyperparameter-search budget (random-search iterations on x-axis). Headline: Trompt is consistently above all DL baselines (SAINT, FT-Transformer, ResNet) on nearly every group, and NARROWS but does not close the gap to tree-based models -- on medium heterogeneous classification it joins the tree leading cluster; on large numerical classification it beats ALL models including trees; on large heterogeneous regression it beats DL by a large margin. Weak spot: large-sized regression with numerical-only features, where it is slightly worse than SAINT/FT-Transformer. Trompt's curves are notably FLAT, i.e. strong at default hyperparameters (it used a SMALLER search space than baselines). Ablations: P=1 prompt collapses performance (clf 79.74% vs 81.81% at P=128) but 64/128/256 are within ~0.1% (insensitive once P is large enough); removing the part-3 feature-expansion Dense drops clf 81.81%->80.76%, reg 74.15%->73.73% (confirms per-prompt feature variation is load-bearing). Interpretability: per-sample importances M_importance recover the ground-truth informative features on TabNet's Syn2/Syn4 synthetic sets and rank 'odor' top-1 on mushroom, consistent with GBDT importances.
baselines_compared: DL: SAINT, FT-Transformer, ResNet (tabular). Tree-based (added by the authors on top of Grinsztajn45): RandomForest, GradientBoostingTree, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost. Supplementary head-to-heads vs FT-Transformer (its chosen datasets, Table 21) and vs SAINT (its chosen datasets, Table 22).
transferable_idea: Decouple feature TOKENIZATION from feature IMPORTANCE: keep the cheap per-feature value-token, but learn a small set of input-independent column embeddings (one per column = the column's 'intrinsic property') and P learned soft-prompt vectors, then compute a per-sample, per-prompt softmax importance over columns via a single batched matmul instead of O(C^2) self-attention. For 70+ heterogeneous features this is attractive: importance cost is O(P x C) not O(C^2), prompts give a built-in multi-modal / mixture view of which columns matter for which sample, and the importances are directly interpretable (column-level, GBDT-comparable). The column-embedding-as-sample-invariant-property idea is the most portable piece.
limitations: Authors-stated: design is admittedly 'unconventional' and 'may not be intuitive or perfect'; on large numerical-only regression it underperforms SAINT/FT-Transformer; on FT-Transformer's own datasets it is only comparable-or-slightly-inferior (notably much worse on covertype/CO: 0.9048 vs FT 0.967) and was NOT hyperparameter-searched in those side comparisons (default config, 100 epochs, results taken from baselines' original papers -- so those tables are not apples-to-apples). Implied: no missing-value, temporal, or high-cardinality handling; minimal scalar numerical embedding; large parameter/activation footprint (P x C x d expansion); supervised-only, no transfer; 'prompt learning' is an analogy, not real prompting/pretraining.
pitfalls: 1) The name oversells: 'prompt learning' here is a learned-soft-prompt + learned-column-embedding importance gate, with NO pretrained backbone and NO transfer/in-context behavior -- do not expect TabPFN-style few-shot from this. 2) The Grinsztajn45 win is on a benchmark that deliberately filters out high-cardinality categoricals and missing values and uses moderate feature counts; the headline 'narrows the gap to GBDT' should NOT be read as evidence it scales to 70+ heterogeneous (esp. high-card) features -- that regime is untested. 3) The supplementary FT-Transformer/SAINT comparison tables are NOT fair: Trompt is un-tuned (defaults, 100 epochs) while baselines are from their own tuned papers, and Trompt is clearly worse on some (CO 0.9048 vs 0.967; AL 0.9317 vs 0.953/0.96). 4) Activation memory: the part-3 expansion materializes a B x P x C x d tensor; with P=128 default this is the dominant cost and bites on wide tables -- the O(P x C) framing hides a large constant. 5) The numerical embedding is just scalar->Dense; pairing Trompt's importance gate with strong numerical embeddings (PLR/periodic) is untested but is the natural upgrade.
relevance_to_topic: A tabular-transformer-route alternative that swaps O(C^2) self-attention for a learned column-embedding + soft-prompt importance gate -- directly relevant to making 70+-feature attention tractable and interpretable, though its high-cardinality, temporal, and very-wide-table behavior is unverified and its numerical branch is naive.
code_available: yes -- no standalone official repo, but a faithful, maintained implementation lives in pyg-team/pytorch-frame: torch_frame.nn.models.Trompt and torch_frame.nn.conv.TromptConv, with examples/trompt.py. Library docs: https://pytorch-frame.readthedocs.io/. (No author-released code located; pyg-team port reproduces the Grinsztajn45 claims.)
